machinery_genes.quote: Homologues of EscC (e.g., YscC, InvG, and HrcC) are the only components of the type III apparatus that are clearly found in the outer membrane.
machinery_genes.note: Gauthier, Puente & Finlay 2003 biochemically localized EscC as the EPEC outer-membrane secretin (SctC family). Also covered as the OM ring in the Gaytan 2016 EPEC review.

machinery_genes.quote: The proteins EscD and EscJ form two concentric rings in the inner membrane (IM)
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016 EPEC/A-E review. EscJ is the SctJ-family IM ring lipoprotein; produced as a pre-protein and Sec-translocated to the periplasm.

machinery_genes.quote: The proteins EscD and EscJ form two concentric rings in the inner membrane (IM)
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016. EscD is the SctD-family inner-membrane ring; has a predicted transmembrane domain.

machinery_genes.quote: EscR, EscS, EscT, EscU, and EscV (EscRSTUV;
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016 names the five-protein EPEC export apparatus EscRSTUV. EscR = SctR export-gate component.

machinery_genes.quote: EscR, EscS, EscT, EscU, and EscV (EscRSTUV;
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016. EscS = SctS export-gate component of the EPEC export apparatus.

machinery_genes.quote: EscR, EscS, EscT, EscU, and EscV (EscRSTUV;
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016. EscT = SctT export-gate component of the EPEC export apparatus.

machinery_genes.quote: a cytoplasmic C-terminal domain of ca. 100 amino acids that undergoes autocleavage
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016. EscU is the SctU-family export-gate protein whose cytoplasmic domain autocleaves; named in the EscRSTUV export apparatus list.

machinery_genes.quote: EscV, the major constituent of the T3 export apparatus,
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016; EscV = SctV-family major export-apparatus protein. Independently characterized biochemically in EPEC as the inner-membrane export gate by Gauthier 2003 (PMID 12761113) and as the substrate/SepL docking platform by Gaytan 2017 (PMID 29277965).

machinery_genes.quote: EscN is thought to be the energizer of the secretion machinery, sharing homology with the F0F1 ATPase and having a putative ATP-binding site.
machinery_genes.note: Gauthier, Puente & Finlay 2003 localized EscN as the EPEC cytoplasmic T3SS ATPase (SctN family). Also named in the Gaytan 2016 ATPase complex (EscN, EscL, EscO).

machinery_genes.quote: The ATPase complex is formed by the EscN, EscL, and EscO proteins
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016. EscL = SctL-family ATPase stalk/stator; evolutionarily linked to the b and delta F1-ATPase subunits and proposed to help form the sorting platform with EscQ and EscK.

machinery_genes.quote: The ATPase complex is formed by the EscN, EscL, and EscO proteins
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016. EscO = SctO-family ATPase stalk, evolutionary counterpart of flagellar FliJ / F1-ATPase gamma subunit.

machinery_genes.quote: the sorting platform might be formed by EscQ, EscL, and EscK
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016. EscQ = SctQ-family C-ring protein forming the cytoplasmic annular structure at the base of the basal body / sorting platform.

machinery_genes.quote: the sorting platform might be formed by EscQ, EscL, and EscK
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016. EscK = SctK family (homologous to Salmonella OrgA, Shigella MxiK); critical for T3 secretion.

machinery_genes.quote: It is presumed that the OM and IM rings are connected through a periplasmic inner rod formed by the oligomerization of the EscI protein
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016. EscI = SctI-family inner-rod protein connecting the OM and IM ring substructures.

machinery_genes.quote: EscF is a component of the TTSS needle in EPEC.
machinery_genes.note: Gauthier, Puente & Finlay 2003 name EscF as the EPEC needle component (SctF family). The needle is a superhelical hollow structure of multiple EscF copies (Gaytan 2016).

machinery_genes.quote: The needle length is strictly regulated by and correlates with the size of a family of proteins named type III secretion substrate specificity switch (T3S4) proteins
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016. EscP is the SctP-family molecular ruler / T3S4 protein controlling the ~23 nm EPEC needle length. Role-naming as the ruler; gene name EscP is the EPEC SctP designation.

machinery_genes.quote: The filament is an extracellular appendage found in the T3S systems of A/E pathogens that functions as an adaptor between the needle and the translocation pore
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016. EspA forms the A/E-specific extracellular filament that extends the needle; apparatus (not effector). The text names EspA as the filament subunit.

machinery_genes.quote: The translocation pore is a protein complex formed by the hetero-oligomerization of EspB and EspD subunits
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016. EspB is a translocon-pore subunit (apparatus per brief: translocon proteins form the host-membrane pore, not effectors).

machinery_genes.quote: The translocation pore is a protein complex formed by the hetero-oligomerization of EspB and EspD subunits
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016. EspD is a translocon-pore subunit (apparatus per brief).

machinery_genes.quote: Elimination of SepL abolishes secretion of the translocators and increases secretion of effectors
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016. SepL is the EPEC gatekeeper (SctW family); controls hierarchical substrate switching. Interacts with export gate EscV (Gaytan 2017, PMID 29277965). Not an effector or regulator.

machinery_genes.quote: In EPEC and EHEC SepL interacts with SepD, whose deletion in A/E pathogens results in the same phenotype as the sepL null mutant
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016. SepD forms the SepL/SepD gatekeeper complex governing the translocator-to-effector secretion switch. Included as apparatus-associated control component, not as a chaperone or effector. (The CesL chaperone of this complex is deliberately excluded.)

machinery_genes.quote: a specialized PG degrading enzyme named EtgA
machinery_genes.note: Gaytan et al 2016. EtgA is the EPEC T3SS-associated peptidoglycan lytic enzyme that clears the PG layer for injectisome assembly. Lowest-confidence apparatus call (accessory enzyme, not a core ring/gate component) but a documented machinery-assembly component, not an effector.

notes: System = the LEE-encoded (locus of enterocyte effacement) type III secretion injectisome of EPEC E2348/69, the archetypal Esc/Sep system of attaching-and-effacing pathogens. The instance label 'prophage_PP6' refers to the genome region where MacSyFinder placed this T3SS in NC_011601.1; biologically E2348/69 carries a single chromosomal T3SS, the LEE injectisome, which secretes the curated effectors of this instance (Tir/EspB-class), so the LEE Esc/Sep machinery is the correct answer key. The founding LEE sequence paper is Elliott et al 1998 (Mol Microbiol), which sequenced the complete LEE of E2348/69 and defined the esc/sep/esp gene set; its abstract is not in PMC, so per-gene role quotes are drawn from two ssign-independent sources on this exact EPEC system: Gauthier, Puente & Finlay 2003 (biochemical localization of EscC secretin, EscN ATPase, EscV export gate, EscF needle in EPEC) and the open-access Gaytan et al 2016 Front Cell Infect Microbiol review of the A/E-pathogen T3SS (names the full EPEC Esc/Sep complement with structural roles). Gaytan 2017 corroborates EscV as the export gate / SepL docking platform. Core apparatus covered gene-by-gene: secretin EscC; IM rings EscJ + EscD; five-protein export apparatus EscR/EscS/EscT/EscU/EscV; cytoplasmic ATPase complex EscN/EscL/EscO; C-ring/sorting platform EscQ + EscK; inner rod EscI; needle EscF; molecular ruler EscP; A/E filament EspA; translocon pore EspB + EspD; gatekeeper SepL + SepD; plus accessory PG-lytic enzyme EtgA. Deliberately EXCLUDED as non-machinery: secreted effectors (Tir, Map, EspF, EspG, EspH, EspZ, NleA etc.; note Tir/E2348C effectors are the instance's curated effectors, not apparatus); translocated/cytoplasmic chaperones (CesT, CesD, CesAB, CesL, the SepL-complex chaperone); and transcriptional regulators (Ler, GrlA, GrlR). Lowest-confidence call: EtgA (accessory lytic transglycosylase rather than a core structural subunit). All quotes are verbatim fragments from the cited PMC/PMC-indexed full texts naming each gene as an EPEC LEE T3SS component.
